entry.title: linux gb memory
entry.content:
This is the command to display system memory in GB in Linux.

grep -E 'MemTotal|MemAvailable' /proc/meminfo | awk '{printf "%s %.2f GB\n", $1, $2/1024^2}'
entry.summary: This is the command to display system memory in GB in Linux.

entry.title: confirm random string
entry.content:
Here’s a nice little script that generates a random string.

User is asked to match the string. If there’s a match, it continues.

If there’s no match, it will exit.

confirm() {
  CHARNUM=6
  CHARSET='A-Za-z0-9'
  # Generate random string
  RANDOM_STRING=$(LC_ALL=C tr -dc $CHARSET </dev/urandom | head -c $CHARNUM)
  printf "%s\n" "Please confirm Code to continue: $RANDOM_STRING"
  read -r -p "Code: " USER_INPUT
  if [[ "$USER_INPUT" != "$RANDOM_STRING" ]]; then
    echo "❌ Code does not match! Exiting!"
    exit 1
  fi
  echo "✅ Code matched!"
  echo "Shutting down server!"
}
entry.summary: Here’s a nice little script that generates a random string.

entry.title: git push script
entry.content:
Here’s my new git push script.

#!/bin/bash

comment=$1
if [[ -z $comment ]]; then
  echo "Usage: $0 'put your comment here'"
  exit
fi

git add .
git commit -am "$comment"
git push

To use the script, simply execute ./gitPush.sh ‘put your git comment here’

The script will add, commit and push it to your remote repository.
entry.summary: Here’s my new git push script.

entry.title: git remote set url
entry.content:
I run Gitea on my desktop. Recently my desktop IP address changed.

I should really set it to a static IP address, but that’s another story.

Doing a git pull and a git push no longer works since the remote URL changed.

To update to a new remote location, simply run the command below.

git remote set-url origin http://192.168.68.105:3000/ulysses/scripts.git

If you use SSH, use the SSH remote link instead of HTTP.
entry.summary: I run Gitea on my desktop. Recently my desktop IP address changed.

entry.title: editing crontab without duplicates
entry.content:
Here’s a neat script that will edit crontab without creating duplicates.

#!/usr/bin/env bash

CRON_ENTRY="0 2 * * * /path/to/script.sh"

EXISTING=$(crontab -l 2>/dev/null || true)

if ! echo "$EXISTING" | grep -Fxq "$CRON_ENTRY"; then
  printf "%s\n%s\n" "$EXISTING" "$CRON_ENTRY" | crontab -
fi
entry.summary: Here’s a neat script that will edit crontab without creating duplicates.

entry.title: baggage limits
entry.content:
Here are the baggage limits for international travel for the following airlines.

Baggage Limits    
Delta Airlines    
Baggage – 50 lbs
Carry On – –
Korean Airlines    
Baggage 20kg 44 lbs
Carry On 10kg 22 lbs
Philippine Airlines (domestic)    
Baggage 20kg 44 lbs
Carry On 7kg 15 lbs
entry.summary: Here are the baggage limits for international travel for the following airlines.

entry.title: keyboard freeze
entry.content:
Another potential fix to my keyboard freezing problem on Linux Mint 22.1 after going to sleep.

Edit Grub.

sudo vi /etc/default/grub

Add i8042.dubkdbd=1 to the GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT.

GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="usbcore.autosuspend=-1 quiet splash i8042.dumbkbd=1"

Update Grub and reboot.

sudo update-grub
reboot

Hopefully that fixes it.
entry.summary: Another potential fix to my keyboard freezing problem on Linux Mint 22.1 after going to sleep.

entry.title: usb commands
entry.content:
Here’s a couple of USB command line tools if you having issues with your USB peripherals.

lsusb
xinput

I have issues with my keyboard and this helps.
entry.summary: Here’s a couple of USB command line tools if you having issues with your USB peripherals.

entry.title: disable usb autosuspend
entry.content:
I use a single keyboard between 2 computer systems (MacOS and Linux). I disconnect my USB keyboard on one system and plug it to another whenever I switch computers. When my keyboard is disconnected from Linux for a very long time, the system no longer recognizes the keyboard when I plugged it back in unless I reboot the system. After a little bit of a research, I found a potential solution to the issue. Essentially the fix is to disable the USB autosuspend option which will need to be added to grub.

Here are the steps:

sudo sed -i 's/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="/&usbcore.autosuspend=-1 /' /etc/default/grub
sudo update-grub
sudo systemctl reboot

Hopefully it fixes the issue.
entry.summary: I use a single keyboard between 2 computer systems (MacOS and Linux). I disconnect my USB keyboard on one system and plug it to another whenever I switch computers. When my keyboard is disconnected from Linux for a very long time, the system no longer recognizes the keyboard when I plugged it back in unless I reboot the system. After a little bit of a research, I found a potential solution to the issue. Essentially the fix is to disable the USB autosuspend option which will need to be added to grub.

entry.title: tmux controls
entry.content:
tmux controls

leader key `
switching windows  
move to left window shift + left arrow
move to right window shift + right arrow
swapping windows  
swap windows to left ctrl + shift + left
swap window to right ctrl + shift + right
switching panes  
move to next pane leader o
move between panes leader left, leader right, leader up or leader down
delete pane leader x
sync & unsync panes  
sync & unsync panes leader y
convert pane into window leader !
reload configuration  
reload tmux config leader r
